message.content:
optimize code size for each optimized assembly function in "test" 
We have 'make test-*' to verify and 'make-size-*' to print size 
Now, review one function a time to see if you could reduce the code size (no algorithm change) 
change -> run test 
iterate all functions

input.description: Explore Makefile targets and assembly files

Be very thorough - read the entire Makefile and list every function that has test/size targets.

input.prompt:
In /home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/libm/machine/riscv, look at a few of the `.s` assembly files to understand common patterns and potential code size optimization opportunities. Read at least 3-4 different assembly files (e.g., sf_sin.s, sf_cos.s, sf_exp.s, sf_atan.s or similar). 

Focus on identifying:
1. Common instruction patterns that could be shortened
2. Redundant instructions (e.g., unnecessary moves, loads that could be eliminated)
3. Stack frame usage - are functions saving/restoring more registers than needed?
4. Any literal/constant loading patterns that could be optimized
5. Branch patterns that could use shorter sequences

This is RV32IM (no FPU, no C extension). The goal is code size reduction without algorithm changes.

1â†’# Makefile for libm accuracy test â€” A/B comparison
     2â†’# Target: RV32IM soft-float (Andes optimized assembly)
     3â†’#
     4â†’# Usage:
     5â†’#   make size-all       - report .text size for each assembly function
     6â†’#   make size-sinf      - report .text size for a single function
     7â†’#   make test-all       â€” build, run opt vs ref in QEMU, diff outputs
     8â†’#   make test-sinf      â€” test a single function
     9â†’
    10â†’TOOLCHAIN = /local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5
    11â†’PREFIX    = $(TOOLCHAIN)/bin/riscv32-elf-
    12â†’
    13â†’CC  = $(PREFIX)gcc
    14â†’AS  = $(PREFIX)gcc
    15â†’LD  = $(PREFIX)gcc
    16â†’
    17â†’ARCH    = -march=rv32imc -mabi=ilp32
    18â†’CFLAGS  = $(ARCH) -O2 -mvh -Wall
    19â†’ASFLAGS = $(ARCH)
    20â†’LDFLAGS = $(ARCH) -mvh -lm
    21â†’
    22â†’QEMU = /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
    23â†’QEMU_FLAGS = -cpu andes-n25 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -semihosting -bios
    24â†’
    25â†’SIZE = $(PREFIX)size
    26â†’NM   = $(PREFIX)nm
    27â†’
    28â†’# Assembly sources â€” single-precision
    29â†’ASM_SRCS = sf_atan.s sf_ceil.s sf_cos.s sf_fabs.s sf_floor.s sf_frexp.s \
    30â†’           sf_ldexp.s sf_modf.s sf_sin.s sf_tan.s sf_tanh.s \
    31â†’           sf_acos.s sf_asin.s sf_cosh.s sf_exp.s sf_fmod.s sf_log.s \
    32â†’           sf_log10.s sf_pow.s sf_sinh.s sf_sqrt.s \
    33â†’           sf_fpclassify.s
    34â†’ASM_OBJS = $(ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)
    35â†’
    36â†’# Assembly sources â€” double-precision
    37â†’S_ASM_SRCS = s_sin.s s_cos.s s_tan.s s_atan.s s_asin.s s_acos.s s_fabs.s \
    38â†’             s_ceil.s s_floor.s s_exp.s s_sinh.s s_cosh.s s_tanh.s \
    39â†’             s_log.s s_log10.s s_sqrt.s s_fmod.s s_pow.s s_frexp.s \
    40â†’             s_ldexp.s s_modf.s s_fpclassify.s s_expm1.s s_log1p.s \
    41â†’             s_rint.s s_round.s s_trunc.s s_fmax.s s_fmin.s s_ilogb.s \
    42â†’             s_nextafter.s s_remquo.s
    43â†’S_ASM_OBJS = $(S_ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)
    44â†’
    45â†’# Note: sf_sincos.c and sf_scalbn.c need picolibc headers, excluded from
    46â†’# standalone size target. Measure those via full meson build.
    47â†’
    48â†’# Single-precision functions (must have test_<name>f.c and sf_<name>.s)
    49â†’FUNCS_SINGLE = sinf cosf tanf atanf asinf acosf fabsf ceilf floorf expf \
    50â†’               sinhf coshf tanhf logf log10f sqrtf fmodf powf frexpf ldexpf \
    51â†’               modff fpclassifyf
    52â†’
    53â†’# Double-precision functions with s_*.s assembly (opt vs ref comparison)
    54â†’FUNCS_DOUBLE_OPT = sin cos tan atan asin acos fabs ceil floor exp sinh cosh \
    55â†’                   tanh log log10 sqrt fmod pow frexp ldexp modf fpclassify \
    56â†’                   expm1 log1p rint round trunc fmax fmin ilogb nextafter remquo
    57â†’
    58â†’# Double-precision functions without assembly (run only)
    59â†’FUNCS_DOUBLE_NOOPT = acosh atanh erf erfc lgamma \
    60â†’                     hypot remainder lrint lround
    61â†’
    62â†’# All functions to test
    63â†’FUNCS = $(FUNCS_SINGLE) $(FUNCS_DOUBLE_OPT) $(FUNCS_DOUBLE_NOOPT)
    64â†’
    65â†’# Test output directory
    66â†’TESTDIR = test
    67â†’
    68â†’# Verbose build output (set V=1 to see brief build progress)
    69â†’V ?= 0
    70â†’ifeq ($(V),1)
    71â†’  Q =
    72â†’  MSG_AS  = @printf "  AS\t%s\n" $@;
    73â†’  MSG_CC  = @printf "  CC\t%s\n" $@;
    74â†’  MSG_LD  = @printf "  LD\t%s\n" $@;
    75â†’else
    76â†’  Q = @
    77â†’  MSG_AS =
    78â†’  MSG_CC =
    79â†’  MSG_LD =
    80â†’endif
    81â†’
    82â†’# --- Pattern rules ---
    83â†’
    84â†’%.o: %.s
    85â†’	$(MSG_AS)$(Q)$(AS) $(ASFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<
    86â†’
    87â†’$(TESTDIR)/%.o: $(TESTDIR)/%.c
    88â†’	$(MSG_CC)$(Q)$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<
    89â†’
    90â†’# For single-precision functions: build opt (with asm) and ref (toolchain only) ELFs.
    91â†’# The asm object name: strip trailing 'f' from func name to get sf_<base>.o
    92â†’# e.g. sinf -> sf_sin.o, log10f -> sf_log10.o, fpclassifyf -> sf_fpclassify.o
    93â†’define FUNC_RULES_SINGLE
    94â†’$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1).o sf_$(patsubst %f,%,$(1)).o
    95â†’	$$(MSG_LD)$$(Q)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)
    96â†’
    97â†’$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1).o
    98â†’	$$(MSG_LD)$$(Q)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)
    99â†’
   100â†’test-$(1): $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf
   101â†’	$$(Q)$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf < /dev/null > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt
   102â†’	$$(Q)$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf < /dev/null > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt
   103â†’	@if diff -q $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt > /dev/null 2>&1; then \
   104â†’		printf "  %-14s PASS\n" $(1); \
   105â†’	else \
   106â†’		printf "  %-14s FAIL (%d diffs)\n" $(1) \
   107â†’			$$$$(diff $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt | grep -c '^[<>]'); \
   108â†’	fi
   109â†’
   110â†’size-$(1): sf_$(patsubst %f,%,$(1)).o
   111â†’	@printf "  %-20s %d\n" $(1) `$$(SIZE) -A $$< | awk '/\.text/ {print $$$$2}'`
   112â†’endef
   113â†’
   114â†’# For double-precision functions with assembly: opt vs ref comparison
   115â†’define FUNC_RULES_DOUBLE_OPT
   116â†’$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1).o s_$(1).o
   117â†’	$$(MSG_LD)$$(Q)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)
   118â†’
   119â†’$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1).o
   120â†’	$$(MSG_LD)$$(Q)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)
   121â†’
   122â†’test-$(1): $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf
   123â†’	$$(Q)$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf < /dev/null > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt
   124â†’	$$(Q)$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf < /dev/null > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt
   125â†’	@if diff -q $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt > /dev/null 2>&1; then \
   126â†’		printf "  %-14s PASS\n" $(1); \
   127â†’	else \
   128â†’		printf "  %-14s FAIL (%d diffs)\n" $(1) \
   129â†’			$$$$(diff $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt | grep -c '^[<>]'); \
   130â†’	fi
   131â†’
   132â†’size-$(1): s_$(1).o
   133â†’	@printf "  %-20s %d\n" $(1) `$$(SIZE) -A $$< | awk '/\.text/ {print $$$$2}'`
   134â†’endef
   135â†’
   136â†’# For double-precision functions without assembly: run only (no opt to compare)
   137â†’define FUNC_RULES_DOUBLE
   138â†’$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1).o
   139â†’	$$(MSG_LD)$$(Q)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)
   140â†’
   141â†’test-$(1): $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf
   142â†’	$$(Q)$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf < /dev/null > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt
   143â†’	@if [ -s $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt ]; then \
   144â†’		printf "  %-14s PASS\n" $(1); \
   145â†’	else \
   146â†’		printf "  %-14s FAIL\n" $(1); \
   147â†’	fi
   148â†’endef
   149â†’
   150â†’$(foreach f,$(FUNCS_SINGLE),$(eval $(call FUNC_RULES_SINGLE,$(f))))
   151â†’$(foreach f,$(FUNCS_DOUBLE_OPT),$(eval $(call FUNC_RULES_DOUBLE_OPT,$(f))))
   152â†’$(foreach f,$(FUNCS_DOUBLE_NOOPT),$(eval $(call FUNC_RULES_DOUBLE,$(f))))
   153â†’
   154â†’# --- Top-level targets ---
   155â†’
   156â†’OPT_ELFS = $(foreach f,$(FUNCS_SINGLE),$(TESTDIR)/test_$(f)_opt.elf) \
   157â†’           $(foreach f,$(FUNCS_DOUBLE_OPT),$(TESTDIR)/test_$(f)_opt.elf)
   158â†’REF_ELFS = $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),$(TESTDIR)/test_$(f)_ref.elf)
   159â†’
   160â†’all: $(OPT_ELFS) $(REF_ELFS)
   161â†’
   162â†’test-all: $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test-$(f))
   163â†’	@echo "Done."
   164â†’
   165â†’size-all: $(ASM_OBJS) $(S_ASM_OBJS)
   166â†’	@echo "=== .text size (bytes) ==="
   167â†’	@$(foreach o,$(ASM_OBJS) $(S_ASM_OBJS), \
   168â†’		printf "  %-20s %d\n" $(basename $(o)) \
   169â†’			$$($(SIZE) -A $(o) | awk '/\.text/ {print $$2}');)
   170â†’	@echo "  --------------------"
   171â†’	@printf "  %-20s %d\n" "TOTAL" \
   172â†’		$$($(SIZE) -A $(ASM_OBJS) $(S_ASM_OBJS) | awk '/\.text/ {s+=$$2} END {print s}')
   173â†’
   174â†’list:
   175â†’	@echo "Targets: all size-all test-all clean list"
   176â†’	@echo "Per-function: $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test-$(f))"
   177â†’
   178â†’clean:
   179â†’	rm -f $(ASM_OBJS) $(S_ASM_OBJS) $(TESTDIR)/*.o $(TESTDIR)/*.elf $(TESTDIR)/*_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/*_opt.txt
   180â†’
   181â†’.PHONY: all size-all test-all clean list $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test-$(f)) \
   182â†’        $(foreach f,$(FUNCS_SINGLE),size-$(f)) \
   183â†’        $(foreach f,$(FUNCS_DOUBLE_OPT),size-$(f))
   184â†’

Here is my analysis of code size optimization opportunities across the assembly files.

---

## 1. Dead `mul` low-word results (highest impact, most pervasive)

The `mulr64` NDS32 idiom was transliterated as `mulhu` + `mul` pairs, but in many cases only the high 32 bits are used. The low `mul` result is immediately overwritten by a `mv` on the next line.

mulhu   x13, x14, x14       # want high word
mul     x12, x14, x14       # low word -> x12
mv      x12, x20             # immediately clobbers x12

```asm

mulhu   x11, x13, x11       # high word -> x11 (this is kept)
mv      x10, x20             # immediately clobbers x10
```

**sf_sin.s lines 65-68:**
```asm
mulhu   x15, x14, x11
mul     x14, x14, x11       # low -> x14
mv      x14, x15             # clobbers x14
mv      x15, x20             # clobbers x15
```

This pattern repeats identically in `sf_cos.s`, `sf_tan.s`, and `andes_fpexexp.s` (lines 76-79). Each dead `mul` is 4 bytes. There are dozens of occurrences across the codebase. The surrounding `mv` instructions that save/restore the clobbered register could also be eliminated if the `mul` were removed.

**Savings per instance:** 4 bytes for the dead `mul` + 4-8 bytes for the associated save/restore `mv` instructions = 8-12 bytes each. Probably 15-25 instances across all `.s` files.

---

## 2. `li x_reg, 2147483648` (0x80000000) repeated everywhere

This constant (sign bit mask) appears 160 times across 100 files. On RV32IM without C-extension, `li` of this value expands to `lui + (possibly addi)` -- at minimum `lui reg, 0x80000` which is 4 bytes, but the assembler may use a 2-instruction sequence (8 bytes) depending on the constant encoding.

A simpler 4-byte alternative for any register:
```asm
lui     xN, 0x80000        # guaranteed single instruction
```

More importantly, within a single function that uses this constant multiple times (e.g., `s_atan2.s` uses it twice, `s_pow.s` four times, `andes_dpexatan.s` four times, `andes_fpexlog.s` five times), the value could be loaded once and kept in a register.

---

## 3. `slti t6, xN, 0` + `beqz/bnez t6` pattern (branch-on-sign)

This 2-instruction idiom for "branch if positive/negative" appears constantly:

```asm
slti    t6, x15, 0           # t6 = (x15 < 0) ? 1 : 0
beqz    t6, .label           # branch if x15 >= 0
```

This is 8 bytes. The equivalent single instruction is:
```asm
bgez    x15, .label          # 4 bytes
```

Similarly `slti + bnez` for "branch if negative" can be replaced by `bltz`. This pattern appears in almost every function. Quick counts from the files read:

- `sf_sin.s`: 3 instances
- `sf_cos.s`: 3 instances
- `sf_exp.s`: 4 instances
- `sf_tan.s`: 4 instances
- `sf_atan.s`: 0 (already uses `bnez` directly for non-sign tests)
- `sf_log.s`: 2 instances
- `andes_fpexexp.s`: 4 instances

**Savings: 4 bytes per instance.** This is likely the single highest-impact change across the entire codebase -- probably 100+ instances.

---

## 4. `sltiu`/`sltu` + `beqz/bnez` for unsigned comparisons

Similar to #3, the NDS32 translation created 2-instruction sequences for unsigned range checks:

```asm
sltiu   t6, x15, 254
beqz    t6, .LBspec          # branch if x15 >= 254
```

This is 8 bytes. RV32 has `bgeu`/`bltu` which can do this in one instruction when comparing against a register. When comparing against an immediate, you'd still need a `li` + `bltu`, so the savings depend on whether the comparison value is already in a register.

For the `sltu t6, xA, xB` + `bnez/beqz t6` pattern (register-register), this is always replaceable by `bltu/bgeu` for 4 bytes saved per instance.

---

## 5. Stack frame over-allocation

- **sf_sin.s / sf_cos.s:** Allocate 48 bytes of stack, save 4 callee-saved regs (x9, x18, x19, x20) + ra = 20 bytes of saves. Only 4 bytes of local storage used (at offset 4). Stack could be 32 bytes (5 saves + 1 local + alignment = 24, rounded to 32).

- **sf_tan.s:** Allocates 64 bytes, saves 5 callee-saved regs + ra = 24 bytes. Uses 4 bytes local. Could be 32 bytes.

- **andes_fpexexp.s:** Uses two separate `addi sp, sp, -16` instructions (lines 19 and 21) for a total of 32 bytes, with two separate epilogue adjustments. This could be a single `addi sp, sp, -32` with adjusted offsets.

**Savings:** Modest -- the `addi` instructions are the same size regardless of the immediate, but the double push/pop in `andes_fpexexp.s` wastes 2 instructions (8 bytes) on extra `addi sp` and could be merged.

---

## 6. `li` + `sub` for `subri` (reverse subtract from immediate)

The NDS32 `subri` instruction does `dest = imm - src` in one instruction. The translation uses 2 instructions:

```asm
li      x17, 126             # subri $r1, $r5, #126
sub     x11, x17, x15
```

This is 8 bytes. On RV32IM there is no reverse-subtract, so this cannot be reduced to one instruction. However, if the same constant is used elsewhere in the function, the `li` could be hoisted. The `li 126` appears in both `sf_sin.s` and `sf_cos.s` (and likely `sf_tan.s`).

---

## 7. Tail call to local label instead of simple branch

Several functions use `tail .LB999` to jump to the epilogue. `tail` is a pseudo-instruction that may expand to `auipc + jalr` (8 bytes) for far jumps. Since these are intra-function jumps to nearby labels, a plain `j .LB999` (4 bytes, same as `tail` when target is near, but `tail` signals a call semantics which may inhibit linker relaxation) should work identically and may assemble smaller in some toolchains.

In practice, for short-range jumps the assembler will relax both to JAL (4 bytes), so this may be neutral. Worth verifying with `objdump` on actual output.

---

| # | Pattern | Per-instance savings | Est. instances | Est. total savings |
|---|---------|---------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| 3 | `slti t6 + beqz/bnez` -> `bgez/bltz` | 4 bytes | 100+ | 400+ bytes |
| 1 | Dead `mul` + associated `mv` saves | 8-12 bytes | 20-30 | 200-300 bytes |
| 4 | `sltu + beqz/bnez` -> `bltu/bgeu` | 4 bytes | 30-50 | 120-200 bytes |
| 2 | Redundant `li 0x80000000` reloads | 4-8 bytes | 20-40 (dedup) | 80-160 bytes |
| 5 | Stack frame consolidation | 8 bytes | 5-10 | 40-80 bytes |
| 6 | Hoisting repeated `li` for `subri` | 4 bytes | 10-20 | 40-80 bytes |

# Makefile for libm accuracy test â€” A/B comparison
# Target: RV32IM soft-float (Andes optimized assembly)
#
# Usage:
#   make size-all       - report .text size for each assembly function
#   make size-sinf      - report .text size for a single function
#   make test-all       â€” build, run opt vs ref in QEMU, diff outputs
#   make test-sinf      â€” test a single function

TOOLCHAIN = /local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5
PREFIX    = $(TOOLCHAIN)/bin/riscv32-elf-

CC  = $(PREFIX)gcc
AS  = $(PREFIX)gcc
LD  = $(PREFIX)gcc

ARCH    = -march=rv32imc -mabi=ilp32
CFLAGS  = $(ARCH) -O2 -mvh -Wall
ASFLAGS = $(ARCH)
LDFLAGS = $(ARCH) -mvh -lm

QEMU = /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
QEMU_FLAGS = -cpu andes-n25 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -semihosting -bios

SIZE = $(PREFIX)size
NM   = $(PREFIX)nm

# Assembly sources â€” single-precision
ASM_SRCS = sf_atan.s sf_ceil.s sf_cos.s sf_fabs.s sf_floor.s sf_frexp.s \
           sf_ldexp.s sf_modf.s sf_sin.s sf_tan.s sf_tanh.s \
           sf_acos.s sf_asin.s sf_cosh.s sf_exp.s sf_fmod.s sf_log.s \
           sf_log10.s sf_pow.s sf_sinh.s sf_sqrt.s \
           sf_fpclassify.s
ASM_OBJS = $(ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)

# Assembly sources â€” double-precision
S_ASM_SRCS = s_sin.s s_cos.s s_tan.s s_atan.s s_asin.s s_acos.s s_fabs.s \
             s_ceil.s s_floor.s s_exp.s s_sinh.s s_cosh.s s_tanh.s \
             s_log.s s_log10.s s_sqrt.s s_fmod.s s_pow.s s_frexp.s \
             s_ldexp.s s_modf.s s_fpclassify.s s_expm1.s s_log1p.s \
             s_rint.s s_round.s s_trunc.s s_fmax.s s_fmin.s s_ilogb.s \
             s_nextafter.s s_remquo.s
S_ASM_OBJS = $(S_ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)

# Note: sf_sincos.c and sf_scalbn.c need picolibc headers, excluded from
# standalone size target. Measure those via full meson build.

# Single-precision functions (must have test_<name>f.c and sf_<name>.s)
FUNCS_SINGLE = sinf cosf tanf atanf asinf acosf fabsf ceilf floorf expf \
               sinhf coshf tanhf logf log10f sqrtf fmodf powf frexpf ldexpf \
               modff fpclassifyf

# Double-precision functions with s_*.s assembly (opt vs ref comparison)
FUNCS_DOUBLE_OPT = sin cos tan atan asin acos fabs ceil floor exp sinh cosh \
                   tanh log log10 sqrt fmod pow frexp ldexp modf fpclassify \
                   expm1 log1p rint round trunc fmax fmin ilogb nextafter remquo

# Double-precision functions without assembly (run only)
FUNCS_DOUBLE_NOOPT = acosh atanh erf erfc lgamma \
                     hypot remainder lrint lround

# All functions to test
FUNCS = $(FUNCS_SINGLE) $(FUNCS_DOUBLE_OPT) $(FUNCS_DOUBLE_NOOPT)

# Test output directory
TESTDIR = test

# Verbose build output (set V=1 to see brief build progress)
V ?= 0
ifeq ($(V),1)
  Q =
  MSG_AS  = @printf "  AS\t%s\n" $@;
  MSG_CC  = @printf "  CC\t%s\n" $@;
  MSG_LD  = @printf "  LD\t%s\n" $@;
else
  Q = @
  MSG_AS =
  MSG_CC =
  MSG_LD =
endif

# --- Pattern rules ---

%.o: %.s
	$(MSG_AS)$(Q)$(AS) $(ASFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

$(TESTDIR)/%.o: $(TESTDIR)/%.c
	$(MSG_CC)$(Q)$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

# For single-precision functions: build opt (with asm) and ref (toolchain only) ELFs.
# The asm object name: strip trailing 'f' from func name to get sf_<base>.o
# e.g. sinf -> sf_sin.o, log10f -> sf_log10.o, fpclassifyf -> sf_fpclassify.o
define FUNC_RULES_SINGLE
$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1).o sf_$(patsubst %f,%,$(1)).o
	$$(MSG_LD)$$(Q)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)

$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1).o
	$$(MSG_LD)$$(Q)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)

test-$(1): $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf
	$$(Q)$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf < /dev/null > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt
	$$(Q)$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf < /dev/null > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt
	@if diff -q $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt > /dev/null 2>&1; then \
		printf "  %-14s PASS\n" $(1); \
	else \
		printf "  %-14s FAIL (%d diffs)\n" $(1) \
			$$$$(diff $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt | grep -c '^[<>]'); \
	fi

size-$(1): sf_$(patsubst %f,%,$(1)).o
	@printf "  %-20s %d\n" $(1) `$$(SIZE) -A $$< | awk '/\.text/ {print $$$$2}'`
endef

# For double-precision functions with assembly: opt vs ref comparison
define FUNC_RULES_DOUBLE_OPT
$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1).o s_$(1).o
	$$(MSG_LD)$$(Q)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)

$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1).o
	$$(MSG_LD)$$(Q)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)

test-$(1): $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf
	$$(Q)$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf < /dev/null > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt
	$$(Q)$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf < /dev/null > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt
	@if diff -q $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt > /dev/null 2>&1; then \
		printf "  %-14s PASS\n" $(1); \
	else \
		printf "  %-14s FAIL (%d diffs)\n" $(1) \
			$$$$(diff $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt | grep -c '^[<>]'); \
	fi

size-$(1): s_$(1).o
	@printf "  %-20s %d\n" $(1) `$$(SIZE) -A $$< | awk '/\.text/ {print $$$$2}'`
endef

# For double-precision functions without assembly: run only (no opt to compare)
define FUNC_RULES_DOUBLE
$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1).o
	$$(MSG_LD)$$(Q)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)

test-$(1): $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf
	$$(Q)$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf < /dev/null > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt
	@if [ -s $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt ]; then \
		printf "  %-14s PASS\n" $(1); \
	else \
		printf "  %-14s FAIL\n" $(1); \
	fi
endef

$(foreach f,$(FUNCS_SINGLE),$(eval $(call FUNC_RULES_SINGLE,$(f))))
$(foreach f,$(FUNCS_DOUBLE_OPT),$(eval $(call FUNC_RULES_DOUBLE_OPT,$(f))))
$(foreach f,$(FUNCS_DOUBLE_NOOPT),$(eval $(call FUNC_RULES_DOUBLE,$(f))))

# --- Top-level targets ---

OPT_ELFS = $(foreach f,$(FUNCS_SINGLE),$(TESTDIR)/test_$(f)_opt.elf) \
           $(foreach f,$(FUNCS_DOUBLE_OPT),$(TESTDIR)/test_$(f)_opt.elf)
REF_ELFS = $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),$(TESTDIR)/test_$(f)_ref.elf)

all: $(OPT_ELFS) $(REF_ELFS)

test-all: $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test-$(f))
	@echo "Done."

size-all: $(ASM_OBJS) $(S_ASM_OBJS)
	@echo "=== .text size (bytes) ==="
	@$(foreach o,$(ASM_OBJS) $(S_ASM_OBJS), \
		printf "  %-20s %d\n" $(basename $(o)) \
			$$($(SIZE) -A $(o) | awk '/\.text/ {print $$2}');)
	@echo "  --------------------"
	@printf "  %-20s %d\n" "TOTAL" \
		$$($(SIZE) -A $(ASM_OBJS) $(S_ASM_OBJS) | awk '/\.text/ {s+=$$2} END {print s}')

list:
	@echo "Targets: all size-all test-all clean list"
	@echo "Per-function: $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test-$(f))"

clean:
	rm -f $(ASM_OBJS) $(S_ASM_OBJS) $(TESTDIR)/*.o $(TESTDIR)/*.elf $(TESTDIR)/*_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/*_opt.txt

.PHONY: all size-all test-all clean list $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test-$(f)) \
        $(foreach f,$(FUNCS_SINGLE),size-$(f)) \
        $(foreach f,$(FUNCS_DOUBLE_OPT),size-$(f))

# Plan: Code Size Optimization of RISC-V Assembly Functions

## Context

The RISC-V libm assembly files were transliterated from Andes NDS32 ISA. The translation introduced mechanical idioms that are suboptimal for RV32IMC. The goal is to reduce code size for each function without algorithm changes, verified by `make test-<func>` after each change and measured with `make size-<func>`.

**Target ISA:** RV32IMC (C extension enabled per Makefile `-march=rv32imc`)

## Optimization Patterns to Apply

### Pattern 1: `slti t6, xN, 0` + `beqz/bnez t6` -> `bgez/bltz` (4 bytes saved each)
The NDS32 translation created 2-instruction sign-test-and-branch sequences. RV32 has direct `bgez`/`bltz`/`blez`/`bgtz` instructions. ~100+ instances across codebase.

### Pattern 2: Dead `mul` instructions (4-12 bytes saved each)
`mulhu` + `mul` pairs where only the high word is used. The `mul` result is immediately overwritten by a `mv`. Remove the dead `mul` and associated save/restore `mv` instructions. ~20-30 instances.

### Pattern 3: `sltu`/`sltiu` + `beqz/bnez` -> `bltu/bgeu` (4 bytes saved each)
Same mechanical translation for unsigned comparisons. Register-register comparisons can use `bltu`/`bgeu` directly. ~30-50 instances.

### Pattern 4: Redundant constant reloads
Same constant (e.g., `0x80000000`) loaded multiple times in one function. Load once, keep in register.

### Pattern 5: Stack frame over-allocation / double push-pop
Some functions allocate more stack than needed or use two `addi sp` instructions where one suffices.

## Execution Strategy

Process functions one at a time. For each function:

1. `make size-<func>` -- record baseline size
2. Read the `.s` file, identify applicable patterns
3. Apply optimizations
4. `make test-<func>` -- verify correctness (PASS)
5. `make size-<func>` -- record new size
6. Move to next function

### Order: Start with single-precision, then double-precision

**Single-precision (22 functions):**
sinf, cosf, tanf, atanf, asinf, acosf, fabsf, ceilf, floorf, expf, sinhf, coshf, tanhf, logf, log10f, sqrtf, fmodf, powf, frexpf, ldexpf, modff, fpclassifyf

**Double-precision (31 functions):**
sin, cos, tan, atan, asin, acos, fabs, ceil, floor, exp, sinh, cosh, tanh, log, log10, sqrt, fmod, pow, frexp, ldexp, modf, fpclassify, expm1, log1p, rint, round, trunc, fmax, fmin, ilogb, nextafter, remquo

## Critical Files

- Assembly sources: `sf_*.s` and `s_*.s` in `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/libm/machine/riscv/`
- Makefile: `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/libm/machine/riscv/Makefile`
- Shared helpers (`andes_*.s`) - read but optimize separately since they affect multiple functions

## Verification

For each function: `make test-<func>` must output PASS.
After all functions: `make test-all` for full regression, `make size-all` for final size report.
